---
title: "SOLVED dual monitors ubuntu cant boot into main monitor"
date: 2013-07-18
forum: New to Ubuntu
---

### Post by arranskye on 2013-07-18
Hi,  My pc has only one vga port, no hdmi or dvi. I wanted a 2nd monitor so i bought a nvidia Quadro 290 PCI graphic card. I understand this is a dual card and it had one connection with a splitter cable for each monitor.  Input the card and udated the drivers all ok.

My set up is: 320GB sata drive running win 7,  120GB ssd drive dual booting win 7 & Ubuntu My on board graphics is IntelG41 chipset.

PC boots to grub  menu, with the options to boot Ubuntu, dev/sda1 or dev/sdb1 The windows OS on both drives work correctly on both monitors, but when I enter Ubuntu from the boot menu  the screen on the main monitor just goes to blue with a no signal box floating around.

I have tried starting Ubuntu with the 2nd monitor switched off but still wont boot up into main 22" monitor. (its a tv) I have used this monitor for some time running Ubuntu with no problems at all.  However Ubuntu does boot up into the 19" HP (2nd monitor) and thats what I am using now.  
This would indicate that the graphics are working ok and for some reason it is not detecting the main monitor.
Or possibly its using the onboard graphics which have no access to dual monitors.

I tried removing the connection to the new graphics card and using the original vga cable & port but still would not boot into Ubuntu.
Please help.  Thanks

---

### Post by zero2xiii on 2013-07-18
Hay,

Not to sound daft but have you simply swapped the screen connectors around?

Secondly, just to be clear, both monitors are connected to the NVS 290 right? Not one on the NVS and the other on the onboard? Do they both work when you are logged in, working correctly?

You can change the default "primary" display, but it should not be necesary if you simply changed the connections around, it should be the most effect fix. If that does not work, then you can alter the primary display:

[http://nxadm.wordpress.com/2011/05/13/define-the-main-screen-in-ubuntu-unity-dual-screen-setup/](http://nxadm.wordpress.com/2011/05/13/define-the-main-screen-in-ubuntu-unity-dual-screen-setup/)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1309247](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1309247)
[http://www.thetechrepo.com/main-articles/502-how-to-change-the-primary-monitor-in-ubuntu-or-other-linux-distributions](http://www.thetechrepo.com/main-articles/502-how-to-change-the-primary-monitor-in-ubuntu-or-other-linux-distributions)

Cheers

---

### Post by arranskye on 2013-07-18
Hi, Thanks for you reply.  tried switching the cables but no difference.  Also tried your suggestions but no joy.  I think because the codes were for changing a monitor to primary or designating a monitor as primary. In this case Ubuntu cant even find the monitor.

Checked in displays and only the HP monitor is showing.  Very new to Linux and scared to death of the terminal but i tried this modprobe nvidia 290.  output none found.  I dont think Ubuntu is seeing the new graphics card. I think it is using the onboard Intel G41 chip, but there is no cable connected to the VGA port on the PC.?????????

I bought this graphic card because it was designed for the purpose of running several  monitors and both monitors should run on this card on its single connection with output via the split cables.

I tried running the original PC vga port to vga monitor port as it has always been, but this did not work. Perhaps because the new graphics card was still installed.[FONT=Arial, sans-serif][SIZE=4]Below is part of the details in the Ebay listing for the NVS card I bought.
 [I]
award-winning NVIDIA Quadro® NVS series is the business standard for multi-display desktop graphics. Combining industry-leading hardware and software, the NVIDIA Quadro NVS series features NVIDIA® nView® multi-display support for single-, dual-, and quad-display graphics products that deliver unprecedented performance and stability.[/I][/SIZE][/FONT][I]

thanks for all your help.  perhaps you could help me with some of the terminal codes to establish if Ubuntu is failing to see or communicate with the NVS 290  please.  thanks
[/I]

---

### Post by newb85 on 2013-07-18
How about giving more specific info on your card?  Please run
```
sudo lshw -C display
```
and post the output (in code tags, please!).

maggie@margaret-VS273AA-ABU-CQ5226UK:~$ sudo lshw -c display

       description: VGA compatible controller

The Tried this:
[www.nvidia.com/object/unix.html]("http://www.nvidia.com/object/unix.html") to get the latest update which was June 2013. Downloaded to download folder.

then did this: *cd Downloads*    *chmod +x NVIDIA-Linux*.run     ***enter**              *sudo ./NVIDIA-Linux*        **tab  then enter**.        Extracted the files for installation but then got this:

You appear to be running an X server; please exit X before

                                       OK

Didnt know how to exit the X server and continue.

### Post by zero2xiii on 2013-07-19
Hay,

See my signature for the "help us help you" link and see the first section about formating output for the forum, you can type the code brackets manualy if for some reason the # button does not work.

Any way, to install graphics drivers manually is a pain. The only way I could install nvidia drivers each and every time correctly (by hand) is by booting to the recovery mode, then drop to a root shell, enter init 3 level (just before the GUI starts to load) and then install the drivers from there. But I recommend trying the proprietary drivers you can install via synaptic, software centre, apt-get or the application in dash called "additional drivers" first.

So open up dash and search for "additional drivers" and open the application, it should search and list a few drivers to install, select the one "recommended" and install that, reboot and see if both screens are then working.

If all this fails (or even break something) we can help you work from a recovery console.

Cheers and good luck, it should be easy though :)

---

### Post by arranskye on 2013-07-19
Hi, Checked out your signature tips.  Very helpful, thanks  Found drivers as below Although the first one is shown as selected now, when I first opened the apt. the nouveau driver had the orange dot.  Please note that after the driver description it says[I] This device is using an alterntive driver.
Also more information below this screenshot
[/I]
[IMG]http://ubuntuforums.org/;base64,[/IMG]

 Found        	 	 	 	   	 	 	 	   jockey provides a user interface for configuring third-party drivers, such as the Nvidia and ATI fglrx Xjockey-kde 0.9.7-0ubuntu13

Found 2 of each of these x.orgxserver-nouveau.display driver.386   &   xorg xserver-nouveau.display driver i386 (debug symbol)
All claim to support nvidia Quadro cards.

Found Firmware for Nvidia Graphic cards.
Nvidia-linux-x86_64-319.32.run from ~downloads  Downloaded this. Install ok till near the very end when an error message showed. characters required editing.
tried to scroll down and immediately everything turned red and white with unreadable text. 

This still shows in my download folder but no longer opens.

Sorry, ive no idea if any of this information is useful or if any of the drivers would be suitable.  Have you any suggestions please.

I do find it really strange that the Nvidia card is working perfectly on this monitor. Is it not odd that different drivers are required just to run an additional monitor.   somehow it appears to me more likely that the monitor drivers have lost Ubuntu especially as  grub boots fine.


Thanks for your help.  Hope not too much of this is utter rubbish.

### Post by zero2xiii on 2013-07-19
Hay,

Sorry I am slightly lost after your last post so I will ask the following, firstly, stop changing settings and stuff and then post the out put of the following:

```
lshw -c video
```

It should create output similar to this:
```
WARNING: you should run this program as super-user.
  *-display               
       description: VGA compatible controller
       product: G98 [GeForce 8400 GS]
       vendor: NVIDIA Corporation
       physical id: 0
       bus info: pci@0000:01:00.0
       version: a1
       width: 64 bits
       clock: 33MHz
       capabilities: vga_controller bus_master cap_list rom
       configuration: **driver=nvidia** latency=0
       resources: irq:16 memory:fd000000-fdffffff memory:d0000000-dfffffff memory:fa000000-fbffffff ioport:cc00(size=128) memory:fe9e0000-fe9fffff
WARNING: output may be incomplete or inaccurate, you should run this program as super-user.

```

The important information is in bold. IF it is also nvidia (instead of for example "driver=i915") please run the following in terminal:

```
sudo nvidia-xconfig
```

This should set the xconfig file to use the nvidia drivers. (You can try this if the driver above is not nvidia, but I doubt it will have an effect).
After this, restart x, simply logout and back in or reboot the computer. If all is successful the command "lshw -c video" above should state the nvidia drivers are configured to be used.

Now in terminal run:
```
nvidia-settings
```

Under "X server Display configuration" (At this point, make sure all displays are connected and powered on and searching on the correct source)
Click on "Detect Displays (Right panel - Lower center)
If everything when oka this far, it should give you your second display. Now you can set it up as you wish.

If it fails early, we need to manually install the latest drivers manually. Head over to the nvidia website and get yourself the correct drivers, it is fairly simply. Download them to an easy to access directory etc /home/user/nvidia. Make sure the directory is empty. After the download completed, make the file executeable if you know how and then WAIT. I will explain you later (when I have time again I am busy tomorrow) step by step how to boot to recovery mode console, get to the correct level of initialization and install the drivers and getting the system to use them instead of the 100's of other that is currently installed but not used. Please do not try to do this blindly as you might ruin the graphics completely and then figuring out what went south and trying to fix it can be a daunting task.

Thanks and good luck, I really hope the above works for you!

---

WARNING: output may be incomplete or inaccurate, you should run this program as super-user.
maggie@margaret-VS273AA-ABU-CQ5226UK:~$ ```

[sudo] password for maggie:

maggie@margaret-VS273AA-ABU-CQ5226UK:~$ nvidia-settings

 * nvidia-settings-experimental-304
 * nvidia-settings-updates
Try: sudo apt-get install <selected package>
maggie@margaret-VS273AA-ABU-CQ5226UK:~$ 
```


OK will try to d/l the correct drivers tomorrow, (after midnight here now) but then I am away sunday till FRiday so I will save the drivers until you have time to help me install/configure.   Will leave it there for now.  
Thanks a million, you're a star        :KS

---

### Post by newb85 on 2013-07-20
You have the noveau drivers selected, and zero2xiii's directions are for the proprietary nvidia drivers.  Go to the Additional Drivers tab of Software & Updates and switch to one of the Nvidia drivers.  Then try zero2xiii's directions again.

---

### Post by zero2xiii on 2013-07-20
> **newb85 said:**
> You have the noveau drivers selected, and zero2xiii's directions are for the proprietary nvidia drivers.  Go to the Additional Drivers tab of Software & Updates and switch to one of the Nvidia drivers.  Then try zero2xiii's directions again.

This is true, however I don't think (I am not sure to be honest) the command will not be found if the drivers are installed, yet not in use. So I think it is save to say the drivers (or at least that package) is not installed. Again, I am assuming this and it might be completely wrong.

But yes I will type a complete guide tomorrow on how to install the "*.run" or "*.bin" file you get from nvidia website, I need to do it step by step myself to make sure all the details are correct.

Cheers

---

### Post by arranskye on 2013-08-05
Hi Zero, just wanted to say a big thank you for all your help, and update the current position:   Did This:

> apt-get nvidia purge

>  apt-get install nvidia-current                       

This got rid of any previous installation attempts and installed the proprietary drivers.
 
Seconds later both monitors working perfectly.

Thanks also to newb for your contribution.  Learned a lot from this as well as getting the monitors working :D

Cheers margaret

---
